I was born a slave, but I did not know it for the first six years of my life.

My father was a carpenter. He was very skilled. People came from far away to hire him. He paid his owner two hundred dollars a year so he could work and manage his own affairs. His greatest wish was to buy his children their freedom. He tried many times. He never succeeded.

We lived together in a comfortable home. My parents protected me so well that I never felt like a piece of property. I had one brother, William, who was two years younger than me. I also had a wonderful grandmother.

My grandmother was a remarkable woman. She worked hard all day for her mistress. Then at night, she baked crackers and sold them. She saved every dollar she could earn. Her goal was to buy her children out of slavery. When her master died, his property was divided — and that included her children. Her youngest son, Benjamin, was sold for $720. He was only ten years old. That loss broke her heart, but she kept working and saving.

When I was six years old, my mother died. For the first time, I heard people say the word "slave" about me. I finally understood what I was.

My mother's mistress took me into her home. She was kind to me. She taught me to read and write — something very few enslaved people ever learned. I sat beside her for hours, sewing and learning. She sent me outside to play when I was tired. Those years felt almost free.

But when I was nearly twelve, my kind mistress grew sick and died. I wept at her grave every day. I hoped — we all hoped — that she had written in her will to set me free. She had promised my dying mother that her children would be safe.

After she died, someone read her will aloud. She had left me to her sister's five-year-old daughter. All our hopes vanished in that moment.

My mistress had taught me the words of the Bible: love your neighbor, treat others as you wish to be treated. But she did not see me as her neighbor. She saw me as property she could give away.

I tried not to feel bitter. She had been kind to me in many ways. She gave me the gift of reading — and that gift, no one could ever take away.

My grandmother's children were all sold at auction after their mistress died. Not one of them escaped. Not even after years of her faithful service and sacrifice.

That is how my childhood ended — with the auction block waiting, and a world that said I was not fully human.
